q: Your orchestrator delegates a 'find every place we call the deprecated chargeCard() API' task to a search subagent. The subagent works, but the orchestrator's context fills up almost immediately and the run aborts. Inspecting the transcript, you see the subagent returned the full contents of all 60 files it opened, not just the matches. What change most directly fixes the reliability problem?

options.A: Raise the orchestrator's max output tokens so it can absorb the larger inbound payload from the search subagent.
options.B: Have the subagent return only matched file paths, line numbers, and call sites — its conclusion, not the raw files.
options.C: Drop the delegation and run the file search inline in the orchestrator itself, so there is no inter-agent payload transfer at all.
options.D: Lower the subagent's temperature so it produces shorter, more focused responses with much less surrounding explanatory prose.

explanation: Subagents should return distilled conclusions, not raw file dumps, to protect the orchestrator's context. B is correct: returning only matched call sites keeps the payload tiny and useful. A treats the symptom and just delays the overflow while raising cost — output tokens don't bound what the subagent sends back anyway. C throws away the isolation/parallelism benefit of delegation and would fill the orchestrator's own context with the same 60 files. D conflates randomness with verbosity — temperature doesn't control whether the subagent dumps whole files.

q: An agent built around a tool-use loop is supposed to keep calling tools until a task is done, then stop and answer. In production it sometimes loops forever, re-calling the same list_files tool with identical arguments dozens of times before timing out. What is the most effective fix?

options.A: Remove the list_files tool entirely so the agent has no way to call it repeatedly and the loop cannot stall on it.
options.B: Give the agent an explicit stop condition, treat a repeated identical call as 'already have it,' and cap iterations.
options.C: Raise the temperature on each iteration so the agent varies its behavior and eventually breaks out of the repeated call pattern.
options.D: Switch to a larger-context model so the loop has far more room to keep running before it exhausts the window and times out.

explanation: Agent loops need a clear termination condition plus a deterministic backstop. B is correct: defining when to stop and capping iterations guarantees the loop ends. A removes a needed capability to mask a control-flow bug. C adds randomness, which is not a reliable termination mechanism — the loop can still repeat. D just lets the runaway loop burn more tokens before failing the same way.

q: A coordinator must produce a competitive analysis that requires (1) scraping three competitor sites, (2) summarizing each, and (3) a final cross-competitor synthesis. The three scrape+summarize chains are independent; only the synthesis needs all three. The current implementation runs everything strictly one item at a time. Latency is 3x higher than necessary. What is the best restructuring?

options.A: Run the three scrape+summarize chains concurrently, then synthesize once all three return — one barrier before synthesis only.
options.B: Keep the chains sequential but simply drop the two slowest competitors so only one scrape+summarize runs and total latency falls.
options.C: Launch all four steps, synthesis included, concurrently from the very start and reconcile any ordering issues after they all finish.
options.D: Merge the three competitors into one large prompt and have a single agent scrape, summarize, and synthesize them in one pass.

explanation: Independent work should run in parallel with a barrier only before the dependent step. A is correct. B sacrifices coverage by dropping competitors. C is incorrect because synthesis genuinely depends on the three results and cannot start before they exist. D discards the parallelism and overloads a single context with all the raw scraping.

q: A teammate proposes wrapping a trivial task — 'read config.json and tell me the value of the region field' — in a five-subagent orchestration 'to be thorough.' The file path and field are already known. How should you evaluate this?

options.A: Approve it, since adding more independent subagents reliably increases the final answer's overall quality even on the simplest, fully-specified lookups.
options.B: Reject the orchestration: a known-file, single-fact lookup should be answered directly, with no decomposition to justify the overhead.
options.C: Approve it, but first require each of the five subagents to read the entire repository so they share full project context.
options.D: Reject it purely on cost, then use one subagent that re-reads config.json ten times and votes on the region value for safety.

explanation: Multi-agent overhead is justified by breadth, parallelism, or isolation — none present for a single-fact lookup. B is correct. A is false: more agents is not strictly better and adds latency and token cost here. C adds pointless work that doesn't help find one known field. D invents a redundant re-read/voting scheme that adds cost without addressing the real point.

q: You expose an MCP server with two tools whose descriptions are 'Get user' and 'Get account'. The agent constantly picks the wrong one, mixing up userId and accountId, which corrupts downstream calls with financial impact. What is the most effective FIRST step?

options.A: Expand both descriptions to state what each returns, the identifier format expected, examples, and when to use one over the other.
options.B: Put a routing classifier in front of the model that parses each incoming user message and pre-selects the tool by matching keywords.
options.C: Merge the two tools into a single get_entity tool that inspects the identifier passed in and decides internally which lookup to run.
options.D: Add a sentence to the system prompt telling the model to be careful and to make sure it always picks the right tool every time.

explanation: Tool descriptions are the primary mechanism for selection, and minimal ones ('Get user'/'Get account') are the root cause. A is the low-effort, high-leverage first step. B is over-engineered as a first move and brittle to phrasing. C is a heavier architectural change than a first step warrants and hides the ambiguity rather than resolving it. D is vague and gives the model no new distinguishing information.

q: Your MCP server needs to give Claude access to a large, slowly-changing product catalog (read-only reference data) and an action that creates a support ticket (a side-effecting operation). How should these be modeled in MCP?

options.A: Expose the slowly-changing product catalog as an MCP resource that supplies read-only reference context the model can pull in on demand, and model the side-effecting ticket creation as an MCP tool with a typed input schema validating the required fields.
options.B: Expose both the catalog and the ticket creation action as tools, since MCP servers can only offer tools and nothing else.
options.C: Inline the whole catalog into the system prompt on every single request, and expose the ticket creation action as a read-only resource.
options.D: Expose the catalog as a tool that returns the entire dataset, and then model ticket creation as a static reusable prompt template.

explanation: MCP resources model read-only context; tools model callable, side-effecting actions. A maps each correctly. B is false — MCP supports resources and prompts, not just tools. C wastes tokens by inlining the catalog every call and mislabels a side-effecting action as a resource. D mislabels both: the catalog isn't an action and ticket creation isn't a static prompt.

q: A process_refund tool occasionally receives a malformed amount (e.g., a string '50.00 USD' instead of a number) and silently refunds 0, hiding the failure from the agent. What design change best improves reliability?

options.A: Define a strict input schema (numeric amount, required orderId), reject malformed arguments, and return a structured error.
options.B: Have the tool coerce whatever input arrives into a numeric value however it best can, stripping currency units, and proceed so the refund flow is never interrupted.
options.C: Catch every error inside the tool and always return {"status": "ok"}, so the agent never has to deal with a failure case.
options.D: Move validation into the system prompt by instructing the model to always send a clean numeric amount with no units attached.

explanation: Strict schema validation rejects bad input at the right layer, and structured errors let the agent recover instead of silently succeeding. A is correct. B risks refunding wrong amounts via silent coercion. C is the silent-success anti-pattern that hid the bug in the first place. D relies on probabilistic prompt compliance rather than enforced validation.

q: Your team requires that prettier runs on every file Claude Code edits, with zero exceptions, before the change is considered done. Developers keep forgetting to ask for it and unformatted code slips into PRs. What is the most reliable mechanism?

options.A: Add a PostToolUse hook in settings.json that runs the formatter after each edit, since the harness executes hooks deterministically.
options.B: Add an 'always run prettier after editing' instruction to CLAUDE.md and trust the model to remember and comply on every single edit.
options.C: Save a feedback-type memory entry reminding Claude to format files, so the reminder persists and resurfaces across future sessions.
options.D: Create a shared /format slash command for the team and rely on each developer to remember to invoke it manually after every file they have Claude edit.

explanation: Deterministic 'always do X after Y' automation belongs in a hook executed by the harness, not the model. A is correct. B and C both rely on the model remembering, which can't deliver a zero-exception guarantee. D depends on a human invoking the command every time, the exact failure mode described.

q: A monorepo has React components, API handlers, and DB repositories, each with distinct conventions. Test files (*.test.tsx) are scattered next to the code they test and must follow one shared testing convention regardless of folder. You want Claude to automatically apply the right conventions based on file path. What is the most maintainable setup?

options.A: Use rule files under .claude/rules/ with glob patterns (e.g. **/*.test.tsx, **/api/**) so conventions attach automatically by path.
options.B: Put every convention in the root CLAUDE.md under clearly labeled headers and rely on Claude to correctly infer which section applies to the file at hand.
options.C: Create one skill per code type under .claude/skills/ and rely on Claude choosing to load the matching skill each time it edits.
options.D: Drop a separate CLAUDE.md into every subdirectory, copying the shared test convention into each folder that contains a test file.

explanation: .claude/rules/ with glob frontmatter applies path-scoped conventions automatically, independent of directory. A is correct. B relies on inference rather than explicit path matching. C relies on Claude choosing to load skills, which isn't automatic by path. D can't cleanly cover test files spread across many directories and duplicates the one shared rule everywhere.

q: Your CI job runs claude "Review this diff for security issues" and hangs forever; logs show it is waiting for interactive input. You also need its output captured to a file for the pipeline. What is the correct approach?

options.A: Run claude -p (the --print flag) in print/non-interactive mode, which processes the prompt without ever waiting for interactive input, writes its complete result to stdout so the pipeline can redirect it to the file the downstream stage expects, and then exits cleanly with a normal status code.
options.B: Set the CLAUDE_HEADLESS=true environment variable before invoking the command so it skips the interactive prompt and runs unattended.
options.C: Pass the --batch flag to the claude command to force a single non-interactive execution that returns its result and then exits.
options.D: Run claude as a long-lived background daemon in the pipeline and screen-scrape its interactive TUI output into the file the downstream stage expects.

explanation: claude -p (--print) is the documented non-interactive mode for CI: it runs the prompt, prints to stdout, and exits cleanly. A is correct. B (CLAUDE_HEADLESS) and C (--batch) reference flags/variables that do not exist. D is a fragile hack that avoids the proper flag and scrapes a UI never meant for capture.

q: A nightly job asks Claude to extract {invoice_id, total, due_date, currency} from PDFs as JSON. ~6% of responses fail to parse: a stray sentence before the JSON, a trailing comma, or a missing field rendered as prose. The downstream system hard-fails on bad JSON. What is the most reliable production fix?

options.A: Define the extraction as a tool with a JSON Schema (typed, required fields, explicit missing-value handling), force structured output, and retry.
options.B: Append 'Return ONLY valid JSON, no other text' to the prompt and keep parsing the model's raw text response as the pipeline does now.
options.C: Raise the sampling temperature so the model is more flexible about its output formatting and much less likely to get stuck on a rigid structure that breaks parsing.
options.D: Split the output into four separate calls, one per field, and concatenate the returned strings yourself into the final JSON object.

explanation: Forced structured output with a validated schema and retry is the reliable production pattern. A is correct. B is the same 'ask and parse raw text' approach already failing at 6%. C makes formatting less consistent, not more. D multiplies cost and complexity and still doesn't guarantee the concatenated result is valid JSON.

q: A long pipeline prompt is ordered: huge system prompt -> 12 few-shot examples -> user message. You notice extraction accuracy for constraints stated only at the top of the system prompt is markedly worse than for instructions placed right next to the examples. What phenomenon is this, and what is the fix?

options.A: Attention dilution — instructions far from the end of a long context carry less weight. Fix: restate critical constraints near the examples.
options.B: Token budget exhaustion — the large system prompt consumed the tokens that were needed to reason. Fix: delete most of the system prompt to free room.
options.C: Temperature miscalibration — a high temperature is making the model ignore the system prompt entirely. Fix: set the temperature to 0 for the run.
options.D: Instruction conflict — the 12 few-shot examples have silently overridden the system prompt's constraints. Fix: remove all of the examples from the prompt.

explanation: This is attention dilution / lost-in-the-middle: content far from the end of a long context gets less weight. A is correct — relocate or restate the critical constraints near the examples or just before the user message. B misdiagnoses it as token exhaustion. C is the temperature misconception. D wrongly blames the examples and would remove useful guidance.

q: A support agent hits only 55% first-contact resolution (target 80%). Logs show it escalates easy cases (standard photo-evidence damage replacements) while trying to autonomously handle complex policy-exception cases. What is the most effective first step to fix escalation calibration?

options.A: Add explicit, decomposed escalation criteria to the system prompt with few-shot examples contrasting 'escalate' versus 'resolve autonomously.'
options.B: Have the agent self-report a numeric 1-10 confidence score on each case and automatically escalate it whenever that reported score falls below a preset threshold.
options.C: Train a separate classifier on historical resolved tickets to predict, before the agent runs, whether each incoming case should escalate.
options.D: Add sentiment analysis to the pipeline and escalate any case where the customer's detected frustration level crosses a high mark.

explanation: Unclear decision boundaries are fixed first with explicit decomposed criteria plus contrasting few-shot examples. A is correct. B relies on poorly-calibrated self-reported confidence — the agent is already wrongly confident on hard cases. C jumps to ML infrastructure prematurely. D conflates customer sentiment with case complexity, which is the wrong signal for escalation.

q: Two Claude-powered workflows currently use real-time API calls: (1) a blocking pre-merge security check developers wait on before merging, and (2) an overnight technical-debt report read the next morning. To cut cost, a manager proposes moving both to the Message Batches API (~50% cheaper). How should you evaluate this?

options.A: Move only the overnight report to batch and keep the pre-merge check on real-time, since batch has no latency SLA (up to ~24h).
options.B: Move both workflows to batch with status polling, since batch turnaround is usually fast enough to cover a developer waiting on a merge.
options.C: Keep both workflows on real-time to avoid batch result-ordering problems that could mismatch responses to the wrong requests.
options.D: Move both to batch but add a timeout fallback that reissues a request as a real-time call whenever a given batch is running long.

explanation: Match each API to its use case: batch is ~50% cheaper but can take up to ~24h with no latency SLA, suiting the overnight job; the blocking pre-merge check needs real-time. A is correct. B is unacceptable for a developer-blocking workflow. C needlessly forgoes savings and cites a non-issue, since results correlate via custom_id. D adds complexity when simple per-workflow matching suffices.

q: A single Claude Code session has run for hours on a large refactor. As the conversation approaches the context-window limit, what does the harness do to let work continue without losing the thread — and what should you (the agent) assume about it?

options.A: It summarizes/compacts the conversation and carries that summary plus remaining context forward, so work continues; don't hand off early.
options.B: It silently deletes the oldest messages as new ones arrive, so you must finish the task and hand off before the limit is reached.
options.C: It automatically raises the underlying model's physical token limit on the fly, so the available context is effectively unlimited and needs no active management at all.
options.D: It terminates the session at the limit and forces the user to restart from scratch, so long tasks should never be run in one session.

explanation: Compaction summarizes the conversation and carries context forward so work continues. A is correct, and you should not wrap up or hand off prematurely just because the conversation is long. B is wrong — recent context is preserved and older context is summarized, not silently dropped. C is wrong — the physical token limit is fixed. D is wrong — compaction exists precisely to avoid a from-scratch restart.
